title: List Methods & Operations 🔧
description: Add, remove, and manipulate list elements.

slides.title: Modifying Lists ✏️
slides.content:
# List Methods

Lists are **mutable** — you can change them after creation:

| Method | Action | Example |
|--------|--------|---------|
| `append(x)` | Add to end | `lst.append(4)` |
| `insert(i,x)` | Insert at position | `lst.insert(0, "first")` |
| `remove(x)` | Remove first occurrence | `lst.remove(3)` |
| `pop(i)` | Remove & return at index | `lst.pop(0)` |
| `sort()` | Sort in place | `lst.sort()` |
| `reverse()` | Reverse in place | `lst.reverse()` |
| `extend(l)` | Add all from another list | `lst.extend([5,6])` |

slides.title: Methods in Action 🎬
slides.content: Watch how list methods modify lists:
slides.codeSnippet:
fruits = ["apple", "banana"]
print("Start:", fruits)

fruits.append("cherry")
print("Append:", fruits)

fruits.insert(1, "blueberry")
print("Insert:", fruits)

fruits.remove("banana")
print("Remove:", fruits)

popped = fruits.pop()
print(f"Popped: {popped}, List: {fruits}")

quizData.question: Which method adds an element to the END of a list?
quizData.explanation: append() adds an element to the end of a list.

slides.title: The `in` Operator 🔍
slides.content:
## Check Membership

Use `in` to check if an item exists in a list:

```
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

print("apple" in fruits)   # True
print("grape" in fruits)   # False
print("grape" not in fruits) # True
```

This works with strings, tuples, and other sequences too! It's one of Python's most readable features.

quizData.question: What does pop() return?
quizData.explanation: pop() removes AND returns the element at the given index (default: last).
